content.text: All organisms in an area, together with the non-living constituents of their environment, form an ecosystem. It is a functional unit where biotic and abiotic components interact to maintain a balance in nature.

content.title: Components of an Ecosystem
items: Biotic Components: Comprising living organisms like plants, animals, and microorganisms.
Abiotic Components: Comprising physical factors like temperature, rainfall, wind, soil, and minerals.

content.title: Types of Ecosystems
content.text: Ecosystems can be Natural (forests, ponds, lakes) or Artificial/Human-made (gardens, crop-fields, aquariums).

content.title: Designing a Human-made Ecosystem (Aquarium)
procedure: Identify requirements: Fish (swimming space), water, oxygen (aerator), and food.
Add aquatic plants and animals to make it a self-sustaining system.
Observe the need for periodic cleaning to remove waste products.
content.observation: An aquarium is an artificial ecosystem that requires external support (like cleaning and food) but can reach a level of self-sustainability with plants.
content.conclusion: Human-made systems like aquariums help us understand how biotic and abiotic factors interact in a controlled environment.

content.text: Organisms are grouped based on how they obtain sustenance from the environment.

rows: Organisms that make organic compounds (sugar/starch) from inorganic substances using solar energy and chlorophyll.
Organisms that depend on producers directly or indirectly for food.
Microorganisms that break down dead remains and waste products into simple inorganic substances.

content.title: Role of Decomposers
content.text: Decomposers act as nature's recyclers by replenishing the soil with minerals found in organic waste, ensuring continuity of life.
A food chain is a series of organisms where each one feeds on the preceding one. Each step or level in a food chain forms a trophic level.

content.title: Trophic Levels
items: First Trophic Level: Producers (Autotrophs) - fix solar energy.
Third Trophic Level: Secondary Consumers (Small carnivores).
Fourth Trophic Level: Tertiary Consumers (Larger carnivores).

content.text: Energy flow involves the transfer of energy from one component of the environment to another, supporting all life activities.

content.title: Key Findings on Energy Flow
items: Terrestrial plants capture only about $1\%$ of solar energy reaching their leaves.
The $10\%$ Rule: Only an average of $10\%$ of the energy from one trophic level is available to the next level of consumers.
Energy flow is UNIDIRECTIONAL: It moves from sun to autotrophs to heterotrophs and does not revert.
Diminishing Energy: Energy decreases at each level, limiting food chains to 3 or 4 steps.

content.title: Food Web
content.text: Most organisms are part of multiple food chains. A food web represents the complex, branching network of these relationships.

Harmful chemicals like pesticides used in agriculture enter the food chain through soil and water. Since these chemicals are non-degradable, their concentration increases at each progressive trophic level.

content.title: Human Impact
content.text: As human beings occupy the top level in many food chains, we accumulate the maximum concentration of these harmful chemicals. This process is known as biological magnification.
Ozone ($\mathrm{O_3}$) is a deadly poison at ground level but performs an essential function in the upper atmosphere.

content.title: Functions and Formation
items: Protective Shield: It shields the earth from damaging Ultraviolet (UV) radiation, which causes skin cancer and other health issues.
Formation: UV radiation splits oxygen ($\mathrm{O_2}$) molecules into free oxygen atoms (O). These atoms then combine with molecular oxygen to form ozone.
Reaction: $\mathrm{O_2 \xrightarrow{UV} O + O}$ and $\mathrm{O + O_2 \rightarrow O_3}$.

content.title: Depletion and Recovery
content.text: Synthetic chemicals called Chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs), used in refrigerants, caused a sharp drop in ozone levels in the 1980s. A UNEP agreement in 1987 led to a freeze on CFC production.
Human activities generate large amounts of waste, categorized as biodegradable or non-biodegradable based on how they interact with biological processes.

rows: Substances broken down by biological processes (bacteria/fungi).
Substances not broken down by biological processes; persist for long periods.

content.title: Decomposition of Waste Materials
procedure: Collect various wastes (peels, paper, plastic, footwear).
Bury them in a pit or bucket and keep moist.
content.observation: Organic matter like vegetable peels and paper change form quickly, while plastics and synthetic materials remain unchanged.
content.conclusion: Biological enzymes are specific in their action and cannot break down most human-made materials like plastics.

content.title: Disposable Progress?
content.text: The shift to disposable items (plastic cups, paper cups) involves trade-offs. While plastic is non-biodegradable, large-scale use of clay cups (kulhads) can lead to loss of fertile top-soil. Paper cups are currently preferred for being more environment-friendly.

glossary.definition: A self-sustaining functional unit consisting of biotic and abiotic components interacting in a specific area.
A specific step or position in a food chain where energy transfer occurs.
Microorganisms like bacteria and fungi that break down complex organic matter into simple inorganic substances.
The progressive accumulation of non-degradable harmful chemicals at each higher trophic level in a food chain.
Substances that can be broken down into simpler entities by the action of biological processes (enzymes).
Substances that are not broken down by biological agents and persist in the environment for a long time.
A triatomic molecule of oxygen that forms a protective layer in the upper atmosphere to shield the earth from UV radiation.
Chlorofluorocarbons; synthetic chemicals mainly responsible for the depletion of the ozone layer.

summary_points: An ecosystem maintains balance through the interaction of biotic (living) and abiotic (non-living) components.
Producers fix solar energy, but only about $10\%$ of it is transferred to the next trophic level.
The unidirectional flow and high loss of energy limit food chains to a maximum of 3 or 4 steps.
Harmful pesticides accumulate in our bodies through the process of biological magnification.
The ozone layer protects life from harmful UV radiation but is threatened by chemicals like CFCs.
Environmental sustainability depends on managing our waste through proper disposal of biodegradable and non-biodegradable materials.
